---
title: "Diagnosis of common skin cancers"
subtitle: "Follow-up remarks for 2025-03-10 meeting"
author: Francois Collin
#date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
bibliography: [Refs/_Derm.bib]
csl: ../_csl/cell-numeric.csl
link-citations: true
always_allow_html: yes
output:
  #html_document:
  # for use of .tabset
  #rmarkdown::html_document:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    code_folding: hide
    code_download: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    # does this have an effect
    fig_caption: yes
    # this has no effect
    number_sections: yes
    # css: ['../_css/pandoc3.css', '../_css/myMargins.css']
---

<!-- 

<p style="background-color: yellow; padding: 5px;">
  <span style="font-weight: bold;">⚠️ Work in Progress:</span> This content is incomplete.
</p>

-->

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE,
                      comment = NA,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      cache = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 8,
                      fig.height = 4)
```


```{r index-GlobalOptions, results="hide", include=FALSE, cache=FALSE}

knitr::opts_knit$set(stop_on_error = 2L) #really make it stop
options(knitr.table.format = 'html')

options(stringsAsFactors=F)

 #knitr::dep_auto()

```
<!-- ######################################################################## -->


```{r index-Prelims,  include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, results='hide', message=FALSE} 


FN <- "index"
if(sum(grepl(FN, list.files()))==0) stop("Check FN")

PREFIX <- "index" #- replace by FLOWCELL???

 suppressMessages(require(rmarkdown))
 suppressMessages(require(knitr))

 suppressPackageStartupMessages(require(methods))
 suppressPackageStartupMessages(require(bookdown))

 suppressPackageStartupMessages(require(magrittr))

 # Shotcuts for knitting and rendering while in R session (Invoke interactive R from R/Scripts folder)
 kk <- function(n='') knitr::knit2html(paste("t", n, sep=''), envir=globalenv(),
       output=paste(FN,".html", sep=''))

 rr <- function(n='') rmarkdown::render(paste("t", n, sep=''), envir=globalenv(),
       output_file=paste(FN,".html", sep='')) ##, output_dir='Scripts')

 bb <- function(n='') browseURL(paste(FN,".html", sep=''))

 # The usual shortcuts
 zz <- function(n='') source(paste("t", n, sep=''))

```
<!-- ######################################################################## -->

<br/>

# Introduction 

* These remarks are offered at the planning stage of a formal validation study.
Before that planning can usefully proceed, it is worth being explicit about
what validation is and is not. 

* Validation verifies the established performance of a finalized,
locked procedure - going into validation we must be confident
in the level of the procedure's performance.

* Having sound QC procedures tuned to the features of the 
test input data, reliable inclusion/exclusion criterion,
and a well undersrtood model selection process are necessary
to ensure that the test performance is stable and reproducible.

* The history of omics-based diagnostic development offers well-documented
examples of flawed products successfully meeting the reqirertmnrts 
of the validation runs, and making it into the clinical settings.
This is precisely why the NCI commissioned
the IOM review that produced the Evolution of Translational Omics guidelines.

* As an aside, it is not a well appreciatyed fact that statistical reasoning
is helpful in the analysis of data all along the data processing pipelines
of omic platforms.  I spend a significant amount of time making sure that
normalization works as expectred, and making sure that poor quality sample
are flagged to alert th downstream analysis modules.
Statistical input is not a validation-stage resource — it is a
development-stage requirement. The purpose of this document is to 
describe what needs to be in place before validation begins.


<br/>

# Data QC - Sample and Run Validity Criteria

* **Before anything else happens**, the nature of the data used by
the test being developed needs to be fully understood.  A data QC procedure 
customized to the specific requirements of the
test in development should be finalized and locked before being applied 
to runs that are part of the test development.

* The samples which are used to fit and validate a model are in many cases more
impactful on the ultimate performance of an omic-based test than the
selected model itself.   It is therefore critically important to apply accurate,
statistically sound Inclusion/Exclusion criteria when selecting samples
for development and validation  (FDA (2019) [@FDA:2019aa]).

* The success of this selection or identification process depends on
having established a comprehensive QC procedure, customized  to the 
specific needs of the test under consideration.
The metrics in Melania et al. (2019) [@Molania:2019aa] to assess
normalization can also be used for QC.

* Through analysis we can determine empirically which are the best metrics to use to identify 
which **specimens** and **analytical runs** are to be excluded.
Inclusions and exclusions must be **pre-specified** as part of the locked procedure.

<!--
* Rules for inclusion/exclusion must be derived empirically from data
collected in the appropriate **operational context** and should be statistically
justifiable.
-->

* This process must be fully documented.

<br/>

## On controls

<p><p/>
* There are various sorts:
   - samples: positive and negative differential expression controls
   - features (miRNAS): stably expressed
   - synthetic miRNA spike-ins from commercial 

<p><p/>
* Control strategies can be implemenred such that various **truths** are built
in every run and can be summuned for QC and normalization.


<br/>

# Model Selection 

* The model selection process should be fully documented to ensure reproducibility.

<p><p/>
* The criteria used to score models must be clearly stated as part of the documentation.
   - the model with the highest cross-validated accuracy estimate is 
**not necessarily the best** model to choose
      - many models may achieve the same accuracy after accounting for estimation error
      - other factors may be equally important:
         - interpretability
         - reproducibility
         - robustness to fitting set selection
         - in a multinomial context, not all error are equal
   
<p><p/>
* In a multi-site context it is important to have a representative set of sites in
the fitting set.

<br/>


# Assay Lock 

A key component of a diagnostic test development
process is Assay Lock.

* Validation only starts **after assay lock**.

* **Lock** means that the complete end-to-end procedure is frozen. 
It must occur *before* any validation begins

* The scope includes: specimen handling, instrument acquisition, 
and all data processing from the instruments output to the final result

<p><p/>
* Every step must be **fully documented** at lock
   - this includes how the model was selected, all QC
metrics and procedures, and acceptance/inclusion criteria.

<p><p/>
* Any post-lock change  constitutes a protocol deviation, and may 
invalidate subsequent validation data

<br/>

<br/>


# NCI IOM Guidelines  {-}


* Following several cases of **flawed products making it into the clinic**,
the National Cancer Institute (NCI) requested that the Institute of Medicine (IOM) 
form a committee to *develop recommendations to clarify and improve the pathway 
from discovery to first use of omics-based tests in a clinical trials*.

> This report identifies best practices to enhance development, evaluation, 
> and translation of omics-based tests while simultaneously reinforcing 
> steps to ensure that these tests are appropriately assessed for scientific 
> validity before they are used to guide patient treatment in clinical trials.

<p><p/> 
* Links to handy resources:
   - [White paper - Evolution of Translational Omics: Lessons Learned and the Path Forward](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24872966/)
      - Guidelines for the Development of Omic Based Products

<p><p/> 
- [Summary slides](OmicsTests_files/_OmicsTests.html)

<br/>

# Discovery Phase Workflows

For the discovery phase, there are three common development workflows.

<br/>

## Recommended Workflow 

The recommended workflow is depicted in Figure \@ref(fig:meet-20250310-disc-rec).


```{r meet-20250310-disc-rec-cap, eval=T}
CAPTION <-  paste(
c("Omics-based test development process: recommended discovery phase"))

```

```{r meet-20250310-disc-rec, fig.cap=CAPTION, echo=F}

   knitr::include_graphics("OmicsTests_files/Omics-based_Test_Development_Process.png", dpi=75)

```

<br/>


### Note on the Confirmation Step {-}

* In the flowchart in Figure \@ref(fig:meet-20250310-disc-rec),
the recommended analysis steps includes a confirmation step 
which is run prior to any formal validation run.  

* The confirmation step can provide added confidence in the expected
results from the validation runs, or may indicate that some tweak to the
test procedure is needed.

<p><p/>
* The use of a confirmation step is highly recommended unless the test data
has been extensively studied in the past and there would be little to 
learn from the results.
   - in my experience the need for a separate confirmation step can
be assessed on a case by case basis.
   - if the training data are complex and representative of the target
population, and the model well understood, and the out-of-fold error rate
similar to the test subset error rate, then the risk of encountering
unexpected and divergent results at validation is limited.

<br/> 

## No-Confirmation Workflow

* The No-Confirmation workflow is depicted in Figure \@ref(fig:meet-20250310-disc-no-confirm).


* The No-Confirmation workflow can be risky, unless the test data are
familiar.

itw
```{r meet-20250310-disc-no-confirm-cap , eval=T}
CAPTION <-  paste(
c("Omics-based test development process: no-confirmation discovery phase workflow"))

```

```{r meet-20250310-disc-no-confirm, fig.cap=CAPTION, echo=F}

   knitr::include_graphics("OmicsTests_files/Omics-based_Test_Development_Process_no_confirm.png", dpi=75)

```

<br/>


## Iterative Model Build Up Workflow

The Iterative Model Build Up workflow is depicted in Figure \@ref(fig:meet-20250310-disc-iterative).

```{r meet-20250310-disc-iterative, fig.cap=CAPTION, echo=F}
   knitr::include_graphics("OmicsTests_files/Omics-based_Test_Development_Process_iterative.png", dpi=75)

```


<br/>

## Validation Figures {-}

* [Comments](Wei_figs/Wei_figs.html)


<br/>


# References 

<div id="refs"></div>


<br/>


```{r, echo=FALSE}
  knitr::knit_exit()
```

###########################################################
 ARCHIVED CODE BELOW
###########################################################
<p><p/>
* The present contains follow-up remarks for 2025-03-10 meeting
   - Maria Wei, Vince Wu, Francois Collin (Me)

<p><p/>
* The remarks are procedural and may not apply, but they are based on my
experience (mostly implementing the ideas of others) and have always
resulted in a smooth path from concept to completion.
   - *Development and Validation of a Multiclass Model Defining Molecular Archetypes of 
Kidney Transplant Rejection* [@Zhang:2024aa] is an example of a test which was 
developed and validated within 18 months.  I was responsible for all design and
analysis.



<p><p/>
* The purpose of the 2025-03-10 meeting was to discuss some validation data
and next steps, but I an not prepared to advise on the validation of a test
I don't fully understand.  It may also be too early to start a 
formal validation process which can only begin after assay lock.
   - There are different notions of *validation*.  The formal validation I am
thinking about here is a validation report which will be filed with a regulatory
agency.  
   - "Validation" outside of the regulatory sphere of influence can mean anything 
from the tabulation of results without a formal analysis plan to analysis made according
to a  pre-specified plan that has been IRB approved and locked.

<!--
<p><p/>
* Now is a good time to plan for validation by making sure an appropriate
number of samples of the right sort will be available for the validation study.
If the samples to be used for validation are already on-hand, they should be
sequestered as they cannot be examined in any way before being used in the
validation study.
-->

<p><p/>
* Before validation begins, the Discovery Phase must be completed
and the assay locked.  The product of the Discovery Phase is a fully
specified test:
   - how the test is carried out, 
   - what are the sample analyzability conditions.  
      - analyzability conditions, or inclusion criteria, come from the Data QC step.


## Knowledge Base {- .tabset}

### Home {-}

<br/>

### AI in Tx Omics  {-}

* Literature search: When do classifiers based on novel methods
do better than traditional methods. 

<br/>

### Biomarkers of Skin Cancer {-}


<br/>

### Classifiers based on micRNA {-}

<br/>


    
<!-- To run
# nohup Rscript -e "knitr::knit2html('index.Rmd')" > index.log  &
    
# Or 
# nohup Rscript -e "rmarkdown::render('index.Rmd')" > index.log  &
    
-->
